attrs.alt: JIT access: standing access exposes 100% of time; JIT reduces exposure to ~5% of active work sessions

content.text: The Case for Just-In-Time Access

Just-in-time (JIT) access is the principle that privileged access should be granted only at the moment it is needed, for exactly the duration required, and then automatically revoked. PIM implements JIT for Azure RBAC and Entra roles. Microsoft Defender for Cloud implements JIT separately for VM management ports.

Attack surface reduction: a credential compromised during standing access immediately grants the attacker full permissions. With JIT, the attacker must also intercept an activation flow that requires MFA and produces an audit event.
Breach containment: when an active PIM session is compromised, the attacker's window is bounded by the activation duration — typically 1-4 hours. Standing access has no such bound.

Compliance evidence: every JIT activation is a discrete, auditable event with a timestamp, justification, and duration. Auditors can trace exactly when privileged access was used and for what stated purpose.
Hygiene: JIT access naturally expires. With standing access, roles accumulate on accounts over time and require manual cleanup. JIT roles expire automatically.

JIT does not eliminate risk — it shrinks the window of exposure and produces an evidence trail. A compromised account during an active PIM session still has the full permissions of the activated role. Combine JIT with short session durations, approval workflows, and monitoring for defense in depth.
Two JIT Implementations in Azure
JIT Type
What it protects
Service
Mechanism
PIM JIT
Azure RBAC roles and Entra directory roles
Microsoft Entra PIM
Eligible role assignment → user activates → time-bounded active assignment
Defender for Cloud JIT VM Access
VM management ports (SSH 22, RDP 3389, WinRM 5985/5986)
Microsoft Defender for Cloud
NSG deny rule → user requests → temporary NSG allow rule for requester's IP

Both types are complementary. A security-conscious VM access pattern uses both: PIM to control who has the RBAC role to manage the VM, and Defender JIT to control which port/IP combinations can reach the VM.
JIT VM Access with Defender for Cloud

Defender for Cloud JIT VM access blocks management ports by default via an NSG deny rule and temporarily opens them when a user requests access. The opening is scoped to the requester's source IP address and a maximum duration.

# JIT VM access in Microsoft Defender for Cloud
# (separate from PIM — applies to VM management ports: SSH/RDP/WinRM)

# Enable JIT on a VM
Set-AzJitNetworkAccessPolicy `
    -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' `
    -Location          'eastus' `
    -Name              'default' `
    -VirtualMachines @(
        @{
            id   = (Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' -Name 'vm01').Id
            ports = @(
                @{ number = 22; protocol = 'TCP'; allowedSourceAddressPrefix = '*'; maxRequestAccessDuration = 'PT3H' }
                @{ number = 3389; protocol = 'TCP'; allowedSourceAddressPrefix = '*'; maxRequestAccessDuration = 'PT3H' }
            )
        }
    )

# Request JIT access to port 22 from your current IP
$myIP = (Invoke-WebRequest -Uri 'https://api.ipify.org').Content
Start-AzJitNetworkAccessPolicy `
    -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' `
    -Location          'eastus' `
    -Name              'default' `
    -VirtualMachineRequest @(
        @{
            id   = (Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' -Name 'vm01').Id
            ports = @(@{ number = 22; allowedSourceAddressPrefix = $myIP; endTimeUtc = (Get-Date).AddHours(1).ToUniversalTime() })
        }
    )

# JIT VM access works by temporarily modifying the NSG rule to allow
# inbound traffic from the requester's IP for the specified duration.
# After the window closes, the deny rule takes precedence again.
JIT VM access requires Microsoft Defender for Servers Plan 1 or Plan 2 on the subscription.
The NSG deny rule has a priority higher than any existing allow rules. JIT request creates a temporary allow rule with priority 100 (or the next available) that overrides the deny.
Requests can be made in the Azure portal (Defender for Cloud → JIT VM access), via PowerShell (Start-AzJitNetworkAccessPolicy), or via az security jit-policy initiate.
Every JIT VM access request is logged in the Azure Activity Log with the requester's identity, IP, port, and duration.
Configuring Approval Workflows

For high-privilege roles, PIM can require approval before an activation becomes active. An approver group is designated per role per scope. The requester submits an activation request, approvers receive an email, and access is only granted after explicit approval.

# Configure PIM approval workflow for a role
# Approval settings are stored in the roleManagementPolicy for the role at the scope.
# This example patches the approval rule to require an approver group.

$scope   = '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
$token   = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token
$headers = @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token"; 'Content-Type' = 'application/json' }

# Step 1: Get the policy ID for the Contributor role at this scope
$roleDefId = (Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor').Id
$policies  = Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET -Headers $headers \
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleManagementPolicies?api-version=2020-10-01"

# Step 2: Find the policy for the Contributor role
# (Each role-scope combination has its own policy)
$policy = $policies.value | Where-Object {
    $_.properties.scopeId -eq $scope
} | Select-Object -First 1

# Step 3: Get the approver group object ID
$approverGroupId = (Get-AzADGroup -DisplayName 'pim-approvers').Id

# Step 4: Patch the approval rule (rule type = Approval_EndUser_Assignment)
# This requires finding the specific rule ID in the policy rules array
# and PATCHing it with the new approver configuration.
# Full implementation involves reading policy.properties.rules,
# finding ruleType = 'RoleManagementPolicyApprovalRule',
# and updating the setting.approvalStages[0].primaryApprovers array.
# Easier to configure via the Entra ID portal for one-time setup;
# use the REST API for automated policy-as-code.
Write-Host "Policy ID: $($policy.name)" # Use this ID for the PATCH request
Approval Configuration
Behavior
When to Use
No approval required
Activation is immediate after MFA + justification
Standard roles (Contributor, Reader) in dev/test
Single approver group
One member of the group must approve
Production Contributor, Subscription Owner
Multiple approver stages
Approvers at stage 1 approve, then stage 2 approvers review
Highly sensitive roles like Global Administrator
Approval with auto-deny timeout
Request is denied if not approved within the configured window (1-24 hours)
All production roles

Set a short approval window (2-4 hours) for time-sensitive roles. If the approver doesn't respond, auto-deny is safer than auto-approve. For truly time-critical operations, consider a 24/7 on-call approver group.
Break-Glass: Permanent Access for Emergency Recovery

JIT is the right default for all humans with privileged access. However, break-glass accounts are a deliberate exception. They hold permanent active assignments specifically because JIT might not be available during a major incident (Entra outage, MFA service disruption).

# Break-glass (emergency) access pattern
# Break-glass accounts bypass PIM — they hold permanent active assignments.
# Purpose: when PIM infrastructure is unavailable (Entra outage, MFA provider down),
# break-glass accounts ensure someone can still administer the tenant.

# Break-glass account requirements:
# 1. Two accounts minimum (geographic separation recommended)
# 2. NOT protected by MFA-requiring Conditional Access policies
#    (because MFA may be unavailable in an outage scenario)
# 3. Password stored in a sealed physical envelope in a secure location
# 4. NOT synchronized from on-premises Active Directory (cloud-only accounts)
# 5. Monitored — any sign-in from these accounts triggers an immediate alert

# Create the break-glass monitoring alert (via Azure Monitor action group)
$breakGlassUpns = @('breakglass1@contoso.com', 'breakglass2@contoso.com')

# Log Analytics query for break-glass sign-in (save as alert rule)
$kqlQuery = @'
SigninLogs
| where UserPrincipalName in ("breakglass1@contoso.com", "breakglass2@contoso.com")
| where ResultType == 0  // successful sign-in
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, Location, AppDisplayName
'@
Write-Host 'Deploy this KQL as a Log Analytics alert rule with 0-minute frequency and immediate notification.'
Write-Host $kqlQuery

# Verify break-glass accounts hold Global Administrator (Entra) AND
# Owner or User Access Administrator at the root management group (Azure RBAC)
# so they can recover both Entra ID and Azure resource administration.
Break-glass accounts must not be protected by MFA-requiring Conditional Access (MFA may be unavailable in the scenario where break-glass is needed).
Any sign-in to a break-glass account is by definition an emergency. Alert immediately via all available channels (email, SMS, Teams, PagerDuty).
Review break-glass accounts quarterly: verify the password is current, the accounts are not expired, and no one has used them non-emergently.
Never use break-glass accounts for routine administration — their use signals a genuine emergency and should trigger an incident review.
JIT for Automated Pipelines

CI/CD pipelines that need privileged access should use PIM-eligible managed identities rather than permanent assignments. The pipeline activates the role at the start of the deployment step and deactivates it immediately after. This gives pipelines time-bounded access with an audit trail per deployment.

# Automate JIT access requests for deployment pipelines
# Pattern: pipeline elevates itself via PIM, runs deployment, deactivates

# The pipeline's managed identity must have an eligible Contributor assignment.
# The pipeline activates it, deploys, then deactivates.

# In Azure DevOps / GitHub Actions — call the ARM API using the managed identity
param(
    [string]$SubscriptionId,
    [string]$RoleDefinitionId,   # Contributor role ID
    [string]$PrincipalId,        # Managed identity object ID
    [int]   $DurationMinutes = 60
)

$scope = "/subscriptions/$SubscriptionId"
$token = (Get-AzAccessToken -ResourceUrl 'https://management.azure.com/').Token

# Find the eligible schedule ID for this MI
$eligible = Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET `
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleEligibilityScheduleInstances?api-version=2020-10-01&`$filter=principalId eq '$PrincipalId'" `
    -Headers @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token" }
$scheduleId = ($eligible.value | Where-Object { $_.properties.roleDefinitionId -like "*$RoleDefinitionId*" })[0].name

# Activate
$activateBody = @{
    properties = @{
        principalId                   = $PrincipalId
        roleDefinitionId              = "$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/$RoleDefinitionId"
        requestType                   = 'SelfActivate'
        linkedRoleEligibilityScheduleId = $scheduleId
        scheduleInfo = @{ startDateTime = (Get-Date -Format 'o'); expiration = @{ type = 'AfterDuration'; duration = "PT${DurationMinutes}M" } }
        justification = "Pipeline deployment run $(Get-Date -Format 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm') — automated"
    }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10

$requestId = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()
Invoke-RestMethod -Method PUT `
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignmentScheduleRequests/$requestId?api-version=2020-10-01" `
    -Headers @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token"; 'Content-Type' = 'application/json' } `
    -Body $activateBody

Write-Host 'Role activated. Running deployment...'
# ... run deployment steps here ...

# Deactivate after deployment completes
# (find active schedule and submit SelfDeactivate request as shown in previous topic)
Write-Host 'Deployment complete. Deactivating elevated role.'
Managed identities can hold PIM-eligible assignments. The activation uses the SelfActivate request type with the managed identity's object ID as the principal.

Pipeline activation does not require interactive MFA — the MFA requirement in PIM policy applies only to human principals. For service principals, configure the policy to not require MFA on activation.
Set a short activation duration (30-60 minutes) — just enough to cover the deployment. If deployment takes longer, build retry logic into the pipeline.
If the pipeline fails mid-deployment, a SelfDeactivate call in the pipeline's error handling block revokes the elevated access immediately.
JIT Combined with VNet and Private Endpoints

In zero-trust architectures, resources are placed behind private endpoints with no public access. JIT VM access is complementary — it provides the temporary network path to the resource when legitimate access is needed.

# JIT access combined with Private Endpoints (VNet-gated JIT)
# Pattern: resources are on private endpoints (no public access).
# JIT grants temporary NSG rule opening for the requester's IP.
# Combined: user must activate PIM role + request JIT port access.

# Step 1 (user): Activate PIM eligible role (Contributor or VM Contributor)
# Step 2 (user): Request JIT VM access
# Step 3 (automatic): NSG rule opens port from user's IP for the duration
# Step 4 (user): SSH/RDP into the VM through the public IP (NSG now allows it)
# Step 5 (automatic): JIT window closes → NSG deny rule takes precedence
# Step 6 (user): PIM role auto-expires at end of activation duration

# Check JIT policy status on a VM
Get-AzJitNetworkAccessPolicy -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' | 
    Select-Object Name, @{n='VMs';e={$_.VirtualMachines.Id}}, @{n='Ports';e={$_.VirtualMachines.Ports.Number}}

# View active JIT access requests
Get-AzJitNetworkAccessPolicy -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Requests |
    Select-Object @{n='Requestor';e={$_.Requestor}}, @{n='StartTime';e={$_.StartTimeUtc}}, @{n='IP';e={$_.VirtualMachines.Ports.AllowedSourceAddressPrefix}}
Conditional Access as a JIT Pre-Check

Conditional Access policies can add requirements at the authentication layer before PIM activation is possible. This creates a layered control: the user must meet CA requirements (compliant device, specific network) before they can even attempt PIM activation.

# Conditional Access + PIM = additional JIT controls at the authentication layer
# CA policies can require specific conditions BEFORE PIM activation is allowed.

# Example: require compliant device for anyone activating Owner role
# This is configured in Entra ID → Security → Conditional Access

# Policy settings (configured in portal, shown here conceptually):
$caPolicy = @{
    displayName = 'Require compliant device for PIM Owner activation'
    conditions = @{
        users = @{ includeRoles = @('Privileged Role Administrator') }  # Or target PIM activation context
        applications = @{ includeApplications = @('797f4846-ba00-4fd7-ba43-dac1f8f63013') }  # Azure Portal app
        signInRiskLevels = @('high', 'medium')  # Block high-risk sign-ins from activating
    }
    grantControls = @{
        operator = 'AND'
        builtInControls = @('compliantDevice', 'mfa')
    }
}
# Deploy via MS Graph:
# New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -BodyParameter $caPolicy

# Authentication context (preview): target CA policies specifically to
# PIM activation requests rather than all Azure Portal access.
# Entra ID → Security → Authentication context → Create context
# Then reference the context ID in the PIM role activation settings.
Authentication context (preview) lets CA policies target specifically the PIM activation flow rather than all Azure portal access, giving precise control.
Require compliant device + MFA for all Owner/Global Administrator activations. This ensures that even if credentials are compromised, the attacker still needs a managed device.
Sign-in risk policies in CA can block PIM activation during high-risk sign-in events (impossible travel, leaked credentials). Entra ID Protection feeds these risk signals automatically.
Named location requirements can restrict PIM activation to specific trusted network ranges — useful for VPN-gated activation.
JIT Access Design Checklist
All privileged roles (Owner, Contributor at subscription scope, Global Administrator, User Administrator) should use PIM eligible assignments — no standing access.
Configure maximum activation duration per role: Owner = 2h, Contributor = 4h, Reader = 8h (or permanent if low-risk).
Require MFA at activation for all roles at subscription scope and above.
Require justification (free text) for all roles; require ticket number for production-scope roles.
Require approval for Owner, Contributor at production subscriptions, and all Entra tenant-wide admin roles.
Set an expiry on eligible assignments (180-365 days) to force periodic renewal and review.
Enable JIT VM access on all VMs accessible from the internet.
Monitor PIM activations via Log Analytics alert rules. Alert immediately on break-glass account use and on activations outside business hours.
Review eligible assignments quarterly via Entra ID Governance access reviews with auto-apply and default-deny-on-no-response.
Summary
JIT access shrinks the privileged access window from 24/7 to only the moments when access is actively needed — reducing the attack surface by ~95%.
Azure provides two JIT implementations: PIM for RBAC/Entra roles, and Defender for Cloud JIT VM access for VM management ports.
Activation controls — MFA, justification, ticket number, approval — are layered requirements configurable per role per scope.
Break-glass accounts deliberately bypass JIT with permanent active assignments and must be monitored with immediate alerting on any sign-in.
Managed identity pipelines can use PIM-eligible assignments with programmatic SelfActivate/SelfDeactivate calls for JIT in automation.
Combine JIT with Conditional Access (device compliance, sign-in risk) for defense in depth at the authentication layer.
